title: Chapter 30: The Seventh Gift
content: The Boundary Stones pulsed with seven heartbeats.

I felt them before I heard them — a rhythm against my palms where I pressed my hands flat against the largest stone, the one Nadine had carved with her own bleeding fingernails the night she died. The pulse traveled up through my wrists, my forearms, settled somewhere behind my sternum like a second heart trying to sync with mine. Six ancestress spirits, each one a steady percussion. And Nadine's — Nadine's was the strongest, the most insistent, a bass thrum that vibrated in my teeth.
Seven gifts. Seven heartbeats. Seven pieces of something I couldn't yet make whole.

The forest around me was quiet in the way forests are after fire — not silent, but hushed, reverent, still deciding what it wanted to become. The smell hit me in layers: char and ash underneath, sharp and acrid, and beneath that, the green smell of new growth pushing up through the ruin. Fern fiddleheads. Poke shoots. The stubborn insistence of life returning to ground that had been burned clean. I breathed it in and it tasted like loss and hope in the same mouthful.

I had all seven gifts inside me. Voice-Binding, cold and precise as a judge's gavel. Prosperity Hexes, warm and golden as old coins. Rage Fire, which lived in my chest like an ember that would never go out. Illusion Weaving, slippery and silver, always threatening to dissolve between my fingers. Identity Recovery, anchor-solid, the one gift that kept me me. Impatience-Curse, which buzzed under my skin like a wasp against glass. And Barrier-Weaving — Nadine's gift, the seventh, the one that had cost her everything and come to me through her dying breath.

Any one of them could have been a weapon on its own. Together, they were seven swords I couldn't figure out how to swing at the same time. The barrier I needed — the contract-breaking barrier, the one that would sever the Hollow Council's hold on Blackwood Vale and every Buena who'd ever lived — required them all. Simultaneously. Interlocking. One machine with seven moving parts, and I was staring at the pieces scattered across the workshop floor with no instruction manual.
I opened the journals.

Not the way I'd read them before — not for the stories, not for the grief and the rage and the desperate love that bled off every page. I'd been reading them like a granddaughter. Now I needed to read them like an engineer.

I spread all seven journals across the ground in front of the Boundary Stones. Marguerite's, with its formal copperplate. Ophélie's, the pages warped and water-stained. Célestine's, dense and precise, her handwriting so small it was almost code itself. Josette's, half-burned, the surviving pages dense with crossed-out calculations. Philomène's, barely a journal at all — more like a sketchbook, every spell rendered as a drawing. Renée's, all passion and exclamation points, her emotion so thick on the page I could practically feel the heat of her temper. And Nadine's. Nadine's, which I could barely stand to look at, because her handwriting looked like mine.

I started with the structure. Not what they wrote — how they wrote it. Where the formulas appeared. What order the components came in. What they emphasized and what they left out, because omission is its own kind of blueprint.
And then I saw it.

It wasn't obvious. It wasn't even intentional, I don't think — not at first. But each ancestress had, in her own way, documented not just a gift but a COMPONENT. A piece of something larger that none of them could see the whole of, because none of them had all seven pieces. They were like blind people touching different parts of an elephant, each one describing the section under her hands with perfect accuracy and no idea that the others were touching the same animal.

Voice-Binding: it wasn't just about commanding obedience. The language of the spells — the structure of the invocations, the legal precision of the phrasing — that was SEALING. Voice-Binding was the sealing mechanism. It bound the contracts, closed the loopholes, made the architecture airtight. You couldn't build a barrier that would hold against the Council without the language that made it legally, magically, structurally BINDING. Marguerite hadn't just left me the ability to command. She'd left me the ability to DRAFT.

Prosperity Hexes: the warmth I felt wasn't just abundance. It was CURRENCY. The curse operated on an exchange principle — that was in Josette's journals, the parts that weren't burned away. Harvesting pain required an investment of energy, and the Council had been investing ours for two and a half centuries. To break that exchange, I had to OUTSPEND it. Prosperity Hexes weren't just about making things grow. They were about generating enough magical capital to fund the foundation of something the curse couldn't afford to maintain.

Rage Fire: power source. Plain and simple. The barrier needed energy, and not the quiet, sustainable kind. It needed the kind of energy that comes from injustice concentrated into heat, the kind that makes steam engines move and revolutions ignite. Célestine hadn't just left me her anger. She'd left me the furnace.

Illusion Weaving: defense. The Council couldn't attack what they couldn't identify. Philomène's gift wasn't deception for deception's sake — it was camouflage, misdirection, the strategic advantage of being invisible while you rebuilt the battlefield under the enemy's feet. Not a weapon. A shield that looked like empty air.

Identity Recovery: this one made my throat tighten when I understood it. Renée's gift wasn't about finding yourself. It was about HOLDING yourself. Maintaining coherence under pressure. You couldn't maintain a barrier that required seven simultaneous operations if you lost yourself in any one of them. Identity Recovery was the keystone that kept the architect from becoming the architecture. Without it, the spell would eat you alive — which was exactly what had happened to the women who'd tried before.

Impatience-Curse: I'd been thinking of this one as a hindrance, a burden, the one gift I'd never wanted. But Ophélie's water-stained pages were clear about it. Some spells — the powerful ones, the kind that reshape reality — take decades to cast. The traditional casting time for a barrier that could hold against a 260-year-old curse was measured in generations. I had minutes. Maybe hours, if the Council was slow. Impatience-Curse wasn't a flaw. It was ACCELERATION. It compressed casting time the way a lens compresses light — dangerously, intensely, but effectively.

And Barrier-Weaving: the seventh gift. Nadine's gift. The one that COMBINED them all. Not another component — the FRAMEWORK. The operating system. The machine that the other six components plugged into.

Each ancestress hadn't just left me a gift. She'd left me an instruction. One piece of the machine, documented in the margins of her life's work, waiting for someone with all seven pieces to finally read the whole diagram.
I stood up so fast the journals scattered. My hands were shaking.
The pieces FIT. Not perfectly — not yet. But the shape was there. Seven components. Seven functions. One machine.
But someone had tried this before. Someone had seen this pattern, or at least part of it. Someone had stood at these same stones with the same desperate hope and the same incomplete understanding.
Nadine.

I picked up her journal and I didn't read it this time. I SAT with it. I let my fingers trace the pages the way she must have traced them, sitting in her cottage at the edge of the woods, surrounded by her failed experiments and her partial successes and the creeping certainty that she was running out of time.
Her spellwork — what Odelia and the others had always called her "failed" spellwork — had never been a failure.
It was a rough draft.

She'd figured it out. Maybe not all of it, but enough. She'd understood that the gifts had to be combined, that each one was a component of something larger. She'd mapped the connections. She'd designed the framework. But she'd been trying to build a seven-piece machine with five working parts and two broken ones. Her Voice-Binding was functional, inherited from Marguerite. Her Prosperity Hexes were golden. Her Rage Fire ran hot enough. Illusion Weaving worked as designed. Identity Recovery — that one she'd struggled with, because losing Martin had cracked her sense of self in ways the gift couldn't fully repair. Impatience-Curse was activated but volatile. And Barrier-Weaving — her own gift, the seventh piece — was INCOMPLETE. She'd never finished developing it. She'd run out of time. She'd run out of life.
Five working parts. Two broken ones. Seven-piece machine.

Of course it didn't work. You can't complete a circuit with broken wires. But the DRAFT was still there — in her journal's frantic last pages, in the scorch patterns on the cottage floor that I'd thought were chaos but were actually DIAGRAMS, in the arrangement of the Boundary Stones themselves, which I now realized were positioned in the exact configuration of a seven-part binding circle.
Nadine hadn't failed. She'd been building a prototype and died before she could finish it.
I was the production model.

I pressed my forehead against the largest stone and felt the seven heartbeats pulse against my skull. For the first time since Nadine's death, I didn't cry. I just breathed. The stone smelled like rain and iron and something older — the mineral smell of the earth's bones, deep and patient.
"Aurelia."
Leo's voice, from somewhere behind me. I didn't turn around.
"You've been out here for six hours," he said. "I brought food."

I turned then. He was standing at the edge of the stone circle with a canvas bag over one shoulder and an expression on his face that was carefully, deliberately neutral. The kind of neutral that meant he was terrified but wasn't going to make that my problem.

He set the bag down and pulled out a pie tin. Apple pie, because of course. Golden latticed crust, cinnamon visible through the slits, the smell of it warm and sweet and impossibly normal against the char-and-green scent of the recovering forest.
"I'm not hungry," I said.
"I didn't ask if you were hungry. I brought food. What you do with it is between you and the pie."

He sat down on a flat stone like it was the most natural thing in the world — a man settling into his environment. Forest ranger to his bones. He cut a slice and ate it with his fingers, juice running down his wrist, not watching me, just existing nearby. Present. The solid fact of him against the shimmering unreality of everything I was trying to hold together.
I sat down across from him. I ate a piece of pie. It was good. It tasted like butter and brown sugar and the specific kind of care that shows up in food when someone doesn't know what else to do.
"The journals," I said. "I figured something out."
He looked at me. Waiting. Not prompting — just ready.

I grabbed a stick and started drawing in the ash between the stones. Seven circles, arranged in the pattern of the Boundary Stones, connected by lines that represented the flow of energy between components. Voice-Binding at the top — the seal. Prosperity Hexes to the left — the foundation. Rage Fire at the bottom — the furnace. Illusion Weaving to the right — the camouflage. Identity Recovery in the center — the keystone. Impatience-Curse running through everything like a nervous system — the accelerator. And Barrier-Weaving as the outer ring, the framework that held it all together.

"Seven pieces," I said. "Each one a component of a single machine. Any one of them is powerful on its own. But they're not meant to work alone. They're meant to work together. Simultaneously. Interlocking." I drew heavier lines between the circles. "And the barrier I need isn't just a shield. It's this." I gestured at the whole diagram. "The whole thing."

Leo studied the drawing. He didn't speak for a long time. His forest ranger brain was working — I could see it in the way his eyes tracked the connections, followed the lines, traced the flow from component to component. He thought in systems. Predator-prey dynamics. Watershed patterns. The way a fallen log becomes a nursery for thirty different species. Interconnection.
"It's like a puzzle," he said finally.
"I don't have time for puzzles."

"You're already doing the puzzle." He pointed at the diagram with a cinnamon-dusted finger. "Each piece is incomplete without the others. And the picture on the box is..." He looked up at the Boundary Stones, at the way they pulsed in steady seven-part rhythm. "The barrier."
My chest went tight. "Yes."
"Aurelia." His voice was quiet. "What happens when you complete the picture?"

I looked at the diagram I'd drawn. Seven circles. Seven connections. One machine. And suddenly — not slowly, not gradually, but all at once, the way dawn breaks over a ridge — I saw it. The complete picture, not the components but the RESULT. What the machine actually DID when all seven pieces ran together.
The barrier wasn't a wall.

Walls are static. Walls block. Walls hold the line until they crack, and the Council had 260 years of accumulated power behind them. Any wall I built would eventually fall. That was the fundamental problem that had defeated every ancestress before me — they were trying to build a wall against an ocean. The ocean always wins.
But this wasn't a wall.
It was a restructuring.

The complete machine — Voice-Binding sealing the structure, Prosperity Hexes funding the foundation, Rage Fire powering the mechanism, Illusion Weaving providing cover, Identity Recovery maintaining coherence, Impatience-Curse accelerating the casting, Barrier-Weaving combining the whole — didn't keep the curse out. It RECODED the curse's energy. It took the trauma-harvest — the pain the Council had been siphoning from Buena women for two and a half centuries — and TRANSFORMED it. The curse fed on pain. That was its engine. Harvest suffering, convert it to power, invest that power in maintaining the contract.
My barrier didn't stop that feeding. It REDIRECTED it.

Closed loop. The Council would push their power at the barrier — the way they always had, the way they'd been doing since 1762 — and instead of penetrating, instead of harvesting, their energy would hit the framework and TRANSFORM. Pain would become protection. Suffering would become strength. The very trauma they'd been extracting would circle back to the people it was stolen from, infused into the boundary itself, making it stronger with every attack. The harder the Council pushed, the more powerful the barrier became.
They wouldn't just fail to get through. Their own power would become my fuel.
The curse wouldn't break. It would be RECYCLED.

I laughed. It came out of me like a bark — startled, incredulous, half-choked with something that might have been joy or grief or both. The sound echoed off the Boundary Stones and came back to me changed, deeper, warmer, as if the stones themselves were laughing with me.
"Are you okay?" Leo asked. Which was rich, coming from the man who'd carefully not asked that for the last six hours.
"No," I said. "I'm the opposite of okay. I'm the most not-okay I've ever been. But I understand something I didn't understand before, and that's — that's a kind of okay. A different kind."

I stood up. The seven heartbeats in the stones were syncing now — not seven separate rhythms but one rhythm in seven voices, the way a chord is one sound made of many notes. I could feel every component clicking into mental place. Voice-Binding: the seal. Prosperity Hexes: the foundation. Rage Fire: the furnace. Illusion Weaving: the camouflage. Identity Recovery: the keystone. Impatience-Curse: the accelerator. Barrier-Weaving: the framework. Seven pieces. One machine. And the machine didn't fight the curse — it ATE it. It took 260 years of harvested pain and fed it back to the people it was stolen from.
I grabbed Nadine's journal and wrote in the margins, in my own handwriting that looked so much like hers it made my eyes sting:
*Six women left me their mistakes. One woman left me her death. And together, they left me the design for a machine that turns the curse's own energy against itself.*
*The Hollow Council has been harvesting Buena pain for 260 years. I'm going to build a barrier that takes that harvest and feeds it back to the people it was stolen from.*
*They think they've been farming us.*
*They're about to find out we've been CULTIVATING.*
I closed the journal. The seven heartbeats pounded through the stones, through the ground, through the soles of my feet and up into my chest — one rhythm, one machine, one weapon.
I had the blueprint. I had the pieces. I had the power.
All seven gifts.

The forest breathed around me — smoke and new growth, ending and beginning, the char of what was and the green of what was coming. The Boundary Stones pulsed. Leo sat quiet in the circle of ash, watching me with his steady ranger's eyes, seeing exactly what I was becoming even if I didn't have words for it yet.
I wasn't just a warrior. I was an engineer. And the weapon I'd designed wasn't a sword or a shield or a wall.
It was a revolution that ran on the enemy's fuel.
I picked up the journals — all seven of them — and I walked toward the stones to begin.
